item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Hey ho. This article makes a lot of small references that could easily be turned into inline links and facilitate further wikiwalking. The mention of MTF Alpha-4 could link to their spot on the MTF list. While GRU-P may not have a GoI hub at this point, the Coldest War canon provides a lot of good flavor to this particular group of interest. Additionally, the references to thaumatology made in the addenda could link to the Thaumatology lecture series which still has a huge sway over magic in the SCP universe. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Mobile Task Force Alpha-4 (“Pony Express”) are to intercept all international shipments to individuals credited in SCP-2577 (See Document SCP-2577-Rho for a complete list) and confiscate any packages ostensibly originating from SCP-2577-A. All confiscated items should be treated as anomalous until confirmed otherwise.

Per standing policy, all investigations into former GRU Division P revenue practices are consolidated under Foundation Intelligence Agency operation GOLD RUSH. Reconnaissance efforts to locate SCP-2577-A have been discontinued, pending IA approval.

To this:

Mobile Task Force Alpha-4 (“Pony Express”) are to intercept all international shipments to individuals credited in SCP-2577 (See Document SCP-2577-Rho for a complete list) and confiscate any packages ostensibly originating from SCP-2577-A. All confiscated items should be treated as anomalous until confirmed otherwise.

Per standing policy, all investigations into former GRU Division P revenue practices are consolidated under Foundation Intelligence Agency operation GOLD RUSH. Reconnaissance efforts to locate SCP-2577-A have been discontinued, pending IA approval.

Changing this:

- Sympathetic thaumaturgic focus for anomalously concealed information based on thematically-relevant code-phrases

To this:

- Sympathetic thaumaturgic focus for anomalously concealed information based on thematically-relevant code-phrases

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know this is a late, late response, but it might help anyone coming to it now.

I wanted to leave the second part as open to interpretation as possible, but I probably just ended up failing to tell a story. It's kind of a bad habit of mine. Here's sort of my general idea:

+ show block
– hide block

Based on this line:

- Thaumaturgic focus for anomalous fund creation

So, SCP-2577-A isn't exactly real. It might be extradimensional, but the rules of its world make so little sense that it's more likely a dramatic fiction. GRU-P either summoned -A into existence or created a tenuous link to it, and used the scrap and valuables from it as a no-strings-attached revenue stream for their covert operations.

If the Foundation theory above is right, GRU-P thaumaturges used sympathetic magic tied in with the epic poem to summon the revenue stream, which caused the fictional world of -A to take on many of the characteristics of the poem. The water tsar isn't actually dancing and smashing those ships, GRU-P just mined the poem for magic and belief to make the summoned fictional world more grounded, while still having some feasible "modern" method for extracting money.

After the GRU-P fell from power, -A had nowhere to send their wares and no resources coming in, and no one paying attention to their story. They may be fictional, but (within the rules of the fiction) they still have to eat. The documentary and shipments are how the fuzzy connection is trying to solve that. The old GRU-P/Soviet struggle plotline wasn't working anymore, so what about reality tv?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, if you've got the resources, it's all CC-BY-SA. You could put it in Burbank and make it about Swedish cars.

As for the original, I assume it was all subtitled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are you making this as a mockumentry? You can afford Perlman but not Russian speaking actors?!?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you're even listening to this, instead of Russia, can I place it in the Canadian North somewhere? It would be weird to have Russians speaking English. Something similar to "Ice Road Truckers."

Edit: It will still be with Russian ships, it takes place in an alternate timeline, near-future, where the foundation does not exist. Canadians and Russians working together, to get rid of outdated ships.

item.title: Youtube Series?
item.content:encoded: Hey man, just saying, I'm making a Youtube series out of this article, and I think that your stuff is amazing. Just want to see a picture of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh wow, yeah, he did! I didn't even think of that!

Maybe not satisfying, but honestly the whole initial image started as a dream. Why I was dreaming about Ron Perlman and broken ships, I really couldn't say.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suspect it's because Ron Perlman played Hellboy, who had a lengthy fight with The Deathless one…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So there is a lot to like in this article for me. The contents of the show remind me of a much more anomalous version of the reality shows that have taken over the History and Discovery channel here in the US, but unlike those shows this one has a concept that is both more terrifying and more entertaining. The statistics that you provided (massive wr ships slamming into the cost line at 900km/h, at least 100 ships arriving every day, etc) made me do a double take as the area in the documentary becomes less of a ship yard and more of a shooting gallery with the world’s largest railgun. Additionally, I though the name of the MTF was pretty clever.

If I did have one nitpick, it would be that the last addenda begins to lose me a bit. What I gathered from that, was that the ships in this documentary are those ships being “smashed upon the sea” by the Tsar’s wild dancing, but I’m not sure. It’s not enough for me to dislike this article, but it left me scratching my head.

Overall +1.

One final question though. Why Ron Perlmen. I’m just curious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The initial premise seems intriguing, but I'm not fully sold on how it is presented. It feels like you're using this to present two different ideas with barely connected threads in one article; a documentary series that's somewhat anomalous in nature, and GRU-P happenings. In fact, I almost didn't really understand how the final addendum connected with the rest of the article, and even now, it just feels like it's there only to be there. Despite the fact that it's interesting, I can't say I'm really sold on the article as a whole.

Here are my tastes in entertainment speaking: I think stopping where you did with presenting Frozen Steel isn't enough. I'd love to know more about this show, where the Foundation could realistically and reasonably know more. I think there's a lot going on you barely touch on or imply that would be entertaining in its own right. Though you risk dragging the article on more, it'd be more of an engaging read as a result. Maybe it could even help establish a stronger connection with what GRU-P is up to and make the second addendum seem less… there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I caught the edge of the Deathless reference but the others passed over my head. Then again, I'm Inupiaq, not Russian.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just thought that it was amusing the variety of things they had. It could probably be changed to make more sense, but that's just the difference between a "good" article (canonically sound, reasonable) and an entertaining article. For a documentary about ballistic ship decommissioning, I personally enjoy it as-is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I'm not sure if you were criticizing or enjoying the IA's speculation. If it came across as wild mass guessing (if that's what you meant), I more wanted to give the impression that despite their best efforts and continued investigation, the Foundation just doesn't know what's going on with either the finances or the book.

If that's problematic, would it help if I marked some of them disconfirmed, to give some evidence of activity?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't help but think that I would absolutely watch reality television about people under threat of annihilation by derelict warships fired at them at over 500 mph. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great procedures and description, but it falls off for me with the last part, just due to my own cultural deafness. I think that these "weird media" SCPs (1989, 1756, 2614) are probably my favorite type of article, and while this doesn't quite match up, it's at least unique.

I also want to point out the Foundations's speculation on what the book meant. It's pretty funny what they think it could be, just based on what they've actually seen. Otherwise C+, with B-.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There is a good base for an SCP here, but it feels like it's building up for something, and then….nothing. Maybe I'm missing something subtle, but the way it is now, it needs more content for it to feel complete.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

To be honest, I'm kinda lost with the backstory here. I have no idea why this exists. I'm too tired from Christmas dinner to fully comprehend.

However, the base idea of a documentary about Russian vessels impacting a "landing strip" every few minutes is just awesome and creative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the mental image of ships falling from the sky on a tundra runway. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rewritten slightly now. I just wanted to indicate that the full document is available while making a historical reference. I figure GREAT GAME in this context covers intelligence operations against GRU-P back when the report was originally published.

Also, since redaction for containment reasons is so common, I thought it would be fun to try out redaction for counterintelligence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's not much of a wow factor in this article, but all the elements mesh well together and work out.

END SELECTED PORTIONS. Full report available from RAISA pending GREAT GAME clearance.

I'm not sure why you had included this bit though…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Happy 25th everyone! New one up, kinda wanted to get this one out of my system.

Thanks to MrWrong, Psul, SirPudding, Hercules Rockefeller, and DreamwalkerFae for solid feedback and sticking with me through rewrites.

Initially based on a dream, and then it kind dove through spycraft and folklore.

Thesis statement(?)...
– hide block

If it was real enough to make money for them, it's real enough to keep making.

The items, per DreamwalkerFae's suggestion, are references to Russian folklore. Mostly Sadko, but the missile is all Koschei the Deathless.

If I've missed anything linguistically or culturally about Russia, I apologize, and can alter things.

EDIT: In case it helps, here's some background of what I was trying to get across. It doesn't have all the answers, but hey:

+ show block
– hide block

- The documentary is the Foundation's only source of knowledge about SCP-2577-A. The only evidence they have for -A's existence is the documentary footage (even though there's no record of it being made) and the objects (which they can't trace).

- GRU-P in the old days had revenue sources for secret projects (acquiring anomalies, bribes, equipment) that the Foundation just couldn't figure out. The Foundation1 uses multi-universal trade through MUTA transit arrays for cash and goods, but they couldn't figure out anything about the GRU's equivalent methods other than it involving a book of folklore.2

- The facts that 2577A 1) has superficial similarity to Russian folklore, 2) is in a remote location inside Russia and 3) apparently produces valuable physical objects dredged up the old GRU-P investigation.

- Unfortunately, with the GRU-P long disbanded/reduced as your canon allows, that trail's pretty darn cold.

Footnotes
1. in my headcanon
2. Note that the Foundation's intelligence serves the Foundation's goals, whereas GRU-P are a sub-set of a dedicated and brutal military intelligence group. Foundation may have more resources, but GRU would have a tradecraft advantage.
